questions.question: Define an array and explain its key characteristics.
keyPoints: Collection of elements of same data type
Stored in contiguous memory locations
Accessible randomly using index
Fixed size in static arrays
Zero-based indexing in most languages

questions.question: Explain the formula for calculating the address of an element in a 1D array.
keyPoints: Address = Base Address + (Index × Element Size)
Base Address is address of first element (index 0)
Index is the position of element
Element Size is bytes required by each element
Works due to contiguous memory allocation

questions.question: What is a sparse matrix? Explain one method of representing it efficiently.
keyPoints: Sparse matrix has most elements as zero
Efficient storage methods: CSR, CSC, COO
COO stores triplets: row, column, value
Reduces memory usage significantly
Only non-zero elements are stored

questions.question: Differentiate between static and dynamic arrays.
keyPoints: Static: fixed size at compile time, allocated on stack
Dynamic: size determined at runtime, allocated on heap
Dynamic arrays can resize when full
Static arrays have faster access, no resizing overhead
Examples: C static array vs Python list/Java ArrayList

questions.question: Explain row-major and column-major order of storing 2D arrays in memory.
keyPoints: Both store 2D array as linear 1D sequence
Row-major: elements of each row stored consecutively
Column-major: elements of each column stored consecutively
Index formula row-major: (i × n) + j for n columns
Index formula column-major: i + (j × m) for m rows

questions.question: How is a polynomial represented using an array? Give an example.
keyPoints: Array index represents exponent of variable
Array value represents coefficient at that exponent
Example: 3x² + 2x + 5 stored as [5, 2, 3]
Index 0→5 (constant term), Index 1→2 (x¹), Index 2→3 (x²)
Efficient for dense polynomials

questions.question: What is a jagged array? How does it differ from a multidimensional array?
keyPoints: Jagged array is an array of arrays
Each inner array can have different lengths
2D array requires same number of columns per row
Jagged arrays save memory for irregular data
Example: int[][] jagged = new int[3][]; jagged[0] = new int[4];

questions.question: Derive the formula for locating an element in a 2D array stored in row-major order. Given a 2D array arr[5][7], calculate the memory address of arr[3][4] if the base address is 1000 and each element occupies 4 bytes.
keyPoints: Row-major: consecutive rows stored in memory
Formula: Address = Base + ((i × n) + j) × ElementSize
Where n is number of columns, i is row index, j is column index
Calculation: Base=1000, i=3, n=7, j=4, ElementSize=4

questions.question: Explain the concept of dynamic arrays. How does amortized analysis work for insertion in dynamic arrays? Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of dynamic arrays over static arrays.
keyPoints: Dynamic arrays resize when full, typically doubling in size
Amortized O(1): most insertions are O(1), occasional resize is O(n)
Total n insertions cost O(n), average O(1) per insertion
Advantages: flexibility, no need to know size in advance
Disadvantages: resize overhead, possible memory fragmentation
Time complexity: Access O(1), Search O(n), Insert O(1) amortized

questions.question: Write an algorithm to insert an element at a given position in a 1D array. Also analyze its time and space complexity. What are the different cases that affect its performance?
keyPoints: Algorithm: shift elements from position to right, insert at position
Check for overflow before insertion
Shift operation: move elements one position right
Time complexity: Best O(1) - insert at end
Worst O(n) - insert at beginning
Average O(n) for random position
Space complexity: O(1) - in-place operation

questions.question: Explain the representation of polynomials using arrays. Write an algorithm to add two polynomials represented as arrays. Analyze the time complexity of your algorithm.
keyPoints: Polynomial represented with coefficient at index = exponent
Algorithm: traverse both arrays, add coefficients at matching indices
Handle different degree polynomials
Time complexity: O(max(m,n)) where m,n are degrees
Space complexity: O(max(m,n)) for result array
Example: P1=3x²+2, P2=2x³+x+5 results in 2x³+3x²+x+7
